---
title: "Overview"
---

**What the self-study period is for:**

- Practice AI-assisted coding on your own, at your own pace
- Get comfortable with the Positron + Copilot workflow before Day 2
- Accumulate specific questions and observations to bring back to the group

## How It Works

The self-study period runs for **2–3 days** between the two sessions.
You work independently through the structured case studies below and document
your experience.

> Share your experience in the Teams channel: ask questions, share screenshots
> of what worked or didn't, and pick up tips from colleagues.

Exercises are designed to be progressively more complex, but feel free to
jump around based on your interests and needs.

- **Example 0** is a quick test of your setup. If you haven't completed it yet,
  start here. All you need are fulfilled [minimal prerequisite requirements](/prerequisites/index.qmd),

- **Example 1** is an introduction to the Positron + Copilot workflow using Stata.
  It's a great place to start if you're new to AI-assisted coding or want to get
  comfortable with the tools. It covers the basics AI tools such as:
  inline suggestions, chat mode, inline chat, and agent mode.
  [Example 1](/selfstudy/example-1.qmd) contains detailed instructions and
  suggested prompts to guide you through the process. Finally, there is an
  [18 min video walkthrough](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=U29xAoivY5Y){target="_blank"}
  in case you want to follow along.

- **Example 2** is a live demonstration of using AI to clean and prepare
  for analysis Mexico's ENIGH survey (2016 and 2018). Full details,
  suggested prompts, and instructions are in [Example 2](/selfstudy/example-2.qmd).

  The exercise follows a structured seven-step workflow:

  1. **Set the overall objective** — name the survey, years, target spec, and language up front
  2. **Define inputs and outputs** — specify unit of observation, file format, and modules
  3. **Create a data dictionary** — capture variable names, types, labels, and missings without sharing raw data
  4. **Gather external metadata** — fetch variable definitions, units, recall periods from official catalogs
  5. **Verify understanding** — produce a mapping table before writing any code
  6. **Develop code iteratively** — generate modular, DIME Wiki-compliant scripts one module at a time
  7. **Verify with an independent review** — audit the cleaning code in a separate AI session

  Its main objective is to demonstrate how versatile AI assistance can be in
  understanding data structure and metadata.

- **Example 3** is a final example with less hand-holding that gives you the
  opportunity to practice AI for data analysis tasks with bare minimum
  guidance. It contains an example of a data analysis from the past and
  asks you to reproduce, revise, and reuse it with the help of AI by adding an
  additional survey year. This is one of the most common tasks. Full details and
  instructions are in [Example 3](/selfstudy/example-3.qmd).

# Supporting materials

- [Global Monitoring Database (GMD) variables description](/methods/gmd-requirements.qmd)
  is a key reference for the Example 2.




<!--
- practices the 3R workflow: take existing analytical code and use
  AI to reproduce it with new data, revise it for a different specification, and
  reuse components in a new context. Full details, suggested prompts, and
  instructions are in [Example 3](/selfstudy/example-3.qmd).

## Case Studies

### Example 0: Test your setup (live)

Example 0 is a live demonstration during the session, but you can also follow
along on your own as a self-study exercise. Full details, suggested prompts,
and instructions are in [Example 0](/selfstudy/example-0.qmd).
The objective of this example is to verify that your software environment is
correctly set up for the course. Each file in this folder runs a small analysis
that exercises a different language or tool. If every file runs without errors,
your setup is ready.

### Example 1: Stata in Positron with GitHub Copilot (self-study)

This introductory example walks you through Positron's AI assistance features
using Stata. Full details, suggested prompts, and instructions are in
[Example 1](example-1.qmd). You will practice:

| Feature | What you learn |
|---------|----------------|
| Inline suggestions | Accepting, rejecting, and customizing AI completions |
| Chat mode | Asking for code generation, revision, and troubleshooting |
| Inline chat | Revising specific code snippets without leaving the editor |
| Agent mode | Automating code execution and debugging |

**What to test:** Create a Stata do-file from scratch using AI assistance. The
file should load data, produce descriptive statistics, run a regression, and
generate plots. If AI can generate, run, and troubleshoot the code in Positron
without manual intervention, the workflow is working.

### Example 2: Understanding data with AI (live)

This example demonstrates how to use AI to harmonize Mexico's ENIGH survey
(2016 and 2018) into an analysis-ready dataset following the Global Monitoring
Database (GMD) specification.

The key principle is a **metadata-driven approach**: a data dictionary serves
as the intermediary between raw data and the AI, avoiding direct exposure of
sensitive data. The exercise follows a structured seven-step workflow:

1. **Set the overall objective** — name the survey, years, target spec, and language up front
2. **Define inputs and outputs** — specify unit of observation, file format, and modules
3. **Create a data dictionary** — capture variable names, types, labels, and missings without sharing raw data
4. **Gather external metadata** — fetch variable definitions, units, recall periods from official catalogs
5. **Verify understanding** — produce a mapping table before writing any code
6. **Develop code iteratively** — generate modular, DIME Wiki-compliant scripts one module at a time
7. **Verify with an independent review** — audit the cleaning code in a separate AI session

**What to test:** Work through the prompts to build a data dictionary, confirm
a variable mapping table, and produce modular Stata scripts for demographics,
income, and consumption. If the AI can correctly map source variables to GMD
targets, apply DIME Wiki coding standards, and pass an independent audit, the
workflow is working. Prompts include a substitution table for adapting to R or
Python.

### Example 3: Reproduce, Revise, and Reuse existing analysis (self-study)

This example practices the 3R workflow: take existing analytical code and use
AI to reproduce it with new data, revise it for a different specification, and
reuse components in a new context. Full details, suggested prompts, and
instructions are in [Example 3](/selfstudy/example-3.qmd).

| Task | What you practice |
|------|-------------------|
| **Reproduce** | Adapt an existing script to run on a different dataset |
| **Revise** | Change model specification, add controls, switch estimators |
| **Reuse** | Extract reusable components (cleaning functions, output tables) for a new project |

**What to test:** Take a provided Stata do-file, reproduce its outputs with a
new dataset, revise the regression specification, and extract a reusable
cleaning routine. If AI can map variables between datasets, update the code, and
produce comparable outputs, the workflow is working. -->
